---
title: "Capstone: reading Wakefield's ABF"
subtitle: "Return to the source — decode Wakefield's equations and the coloc interface, then assemble and validate the whole method"
engine: knitr
---

{{< include /_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

```{r setup}
#| include: false
# Build-time setup only — the live cells below re-state their own data. Base R only.
needed <- c()
to_install <- setdiff(needed, rownames(installed.packages()))
if (length(to_install) > 0) install.packages(to_install)
invisible(lapply(needed, library, character.only = TRUE))
set.seed(42)
```

::: {.callout-tip title="This lesson runs in your browser" collapse="true"}
The R cells below with a **Run** button are live (WebR) — you assemble and validate the whole
method right here. The real `coloc` package can't run in the browser (WebR has no `coloc`), so
its interface is shown for reading under a note, with the reference numbers embedded inline.
:::

## Where we're going

No new ideas here — this capstone proves you reached the goal. When you started, the sentence
"we computed an approximate Bayes factor for each SNP" and the closed form
$\text{ABF} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - r}}\exp(-\tfrac{Z^2}{2}r)$ were a wall. Now you've **built**
every piece: the summary pair $(\hat\beta, V)$, the two normal curves, the shrinkage $r$, the
closed-form log Bayes factor, and the posterior probability of association. So we'll return to
the source — decode Wakefield's own equations (including his reciprocal sign convention) and
the `coloc::approx.bf.estimates` / `coloc.abf` interface, line by line, tracing each term to
the module that built it. Then we'll assemble the full `wakefield_abf()`, run it on a simulated
multi-SNP GWAS region, and validate it one last time against the frozen `coloc` reference.

**Builds on:** every module — [the summary pair (1)](../01-the-summary-of-an-association/lesson.qmd),
[the prior and two curves (2)](../02-a-prior-on-the-effect/lesson.qmd),
[the closed-form ABF (3)](../03-the-approximate-bayes-factor/lesson.qmd), and
[the posterior probability (4)](../04-posterior-probability-of-association/lesson.qmd), plus
the foundations they rest on.

## Warm-up: do you remember?

Four recalls spanning the course. Produce each before opening it.

**1.** *(Module 1)* A variant reports $\hat\beta = 0.21$ and $\text{se} = 0.05$. What are $V$
and $Z$?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$V = 0.05^2 = 0.0025$; $Z = 0.21/0.05 = 4.2$. See
[Module 1](../01-the-summary-of-an-association/lesson.qmd).
:::

**2.** *(Module 2)* With prior variance $W = 0.04$, what are the two competing curves for the
estimate?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
Null $N(0, V) = N(0, 0.0025)$ and alternative $N(0, V + W) = N(0, 0.0425)$. See
[Module 2](../02-a-prior-on-the-effect/lesson.qmd).
:::

**3.** *(Module 3)* Write the coloc-convention log ABF in terms of $r$ and $Z$.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$\text{lABF} = \tfrac12\big(\log(1 - r) + r Z^2\big)$ with $r = W/(W + V)$. See
[Module 3](../03-the-approximate-bayes-factor/lesson.qmd).
:::

**4.** *(Module 4)* How do you turn an lABF into a posterior probability of association?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
Posterior odds $= \tfrac{\pi}{1-\pi} \times e^{\text{lABF}}$, then PPA $=$ posterior odds
$/(1 + \text{posterior odds})$. See
[Module 4](../04-posterior-probability-of-association/lesson.qmd).
:::

## The artifact, part 1 — Wakefield's own equations

Here is the ABF as Wakefield (2009) states it. Read it aloud, then we decode every symbol.

> With $\hat\beta$ the estimated log odds ratio, $V$ its variance, $Z = \hat\beta/\sqrt{V}$
> the usual z-statistic, and a prior $\theta \sim N(0, W)$ on the true log odds ratio, the
> approximate Bayes factor is
> $$
> \text{ABF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - r}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{Z^2}{2}\,r\right),
> \qquad r = \frac{W}{V + W}.
> $$

### The decode, term by term

Read each piece and trace it home to the module that built it.

**"$\hat\beta$ the estimated log odds ratio, $V$ its variance, $Z = \hat\beta/\sqrt{V}$."**
This is exactly the summary pair from [Module 1](../01-the-summary-of-an-association/lesson.qmd):
the effect estimate (a log odds ratio, from the
[logistic-regression course](../../../logistic-regression/syllabus.md)), its variance
$V = \text{se}^2$, and the z-statistic. The entire method starts from $(\hat\beta, V)$ — no
raw data.

**"a prior $\theta \sim N(0, W)$ on the true log odds ratio."** The normal prior from
[Module 2](../02-a-prior-on-the-effect/lesson.qmd): centered at zero (no effect is the
default), spread $W$ (how big a true effect could plausibly be). The case-control default is
$\sqrt{W} = 0.2$, i.e. $W = 0.04$.

**"$r = W/(V + W)$."** The shrinkage factor from
[Module 3](../03-the-approximate-bayes-factor/lesson.qmd) — a variance ratio between 0 and 1,
*not* a correlation. It measures how much of the total variance is prior versus noise.

**"$\text{ABF} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - r}}\exp(-\tfrac{Z^2}{2}r)$."** This is the same content
as your Module-3 formula, but written in **Wakefield's own sign convention** — and this is the
one subtlety worth pausing on. Wakefield defines his ABF as the **null over the alternative**
(the *reciprocal* of the coloc convention). Watch the two line up. Take the log of Wakefield's
ABF:
$$
\log(\text{ABF}_{\text{Wakefield}}) = -\tfrac12\log(1 - r) - \tfrac{Z^2}{2}r
= -\underbrace{\tfrac12\big(\log(1 - r) + r Z^2\big)}_{\text{your Module-3 lABF}}
= -\,\text{lABF}.
$$
So Wakefield's ABF is $e^{-\text{lABF}}$ — the reciprocal of the Bayes factor you built.
The consequence for reading the literature: **in Wakefield's convention a *small* ABF means
*strong* association** (the null is far less likely than the alternative), whereas in coloc's
convention a *large* lABF means strong association. Same evidence, flipped sign. `coloc` uses
the coloc convention (large = strong), and so does everything you built.

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
# LIVE: confirm the two sign conventions are exact reciprocals on the log scale.
# TRY IT: change Z and W -- Wakefield's log-ABF is always MINUS your coloc lABF.
Z <- 4.2; V <- 0.0025; W <- 0.04; r <- W / (W + V)

coloc_lABF     <- 0.5 * (log(1 - r) + r * Z^2)          # coloc: large = strong (Module 3)
wakefield_ABF  <- (1 / sqrt(1 - r)) * exp(-Z^2 / 2 * r)  # Wakefield: small = strong
c(coloc_lABF = coloc_lABF,
  log_wakefield_ABF = log(wakefield_ABF),                # equals -coloc_lABF
  reciprocal_check = exp(coloc_lABF) * wakefield_ABF)     # ~1: they are reciprocals
```

## The artifact, part 2 — the `coloc` interface

Now the software. `coloc` computes the same lABF you built, then feeds it into a
regional posterior. Here is the shape of the two functions, shown for reading (they can't run
in the browser).

::: {.callout-note title="Runs on your machine, not here"}
These use the **`coloc` package** (GPL-3), which isn't in the browser's WebR build. The code is
shown for decoding, not running; copy it into your own R session with `coloc` installed. The
reference numbers your self-check uses (below) were produced by exactly this machinery
(`coloc::coloc.abf` 5.2.3) and frozen into `equivalence/fixtures/`.
:::

```r
# --- coloc's per-SNP ABF (the core you rebuilt), shown for reference only ---
# z = beta / sqrt(V), V the variance; type "cc" = case-control; returns a data.frame
# whose lABF column is EXACTLY your wakefield_abf() output (coloc sign convention).
coloc:::approx.bf.estimates(z = beta / sqrt(V), V = V, type = "cc", sdY = 1, suffix = NULL)

# --- coloc.abf: the whole regional pipeline, shown for reference only ---
# Given two studies' per-SNP (beta, varbeta), coloc.abf():
#   1. computes each SNP's lABF (your Module 3),
#   2. turns lABFs into per-SNP posterior probabilities WITHIN the region, and
#   3. combines the two studies into the five colocalisation hypotheses (H0..H4).
coloc.abf(dataset1 = list(beta = b1, varbeta = v1, type = "cc", s = 0.5, N = n1, snp = ids),
          dataset2 = list(beta = b2, varbeta = v2, type = "cc", s = 0.5, N = n2, snp = ids))
```

### The decode, argument by argument

- **`z = beta / sqrt(V)`** — your z-statistic $Z = \hat\beta/\sqrt V$
  ([Module 1](../01-the-summary-of-an-association/lesson.qmd)).
- **`V`** — the variance of the estimate, $\text{se}^2$
  ([Module 1](../01-the-summary-of-an-association/lesson.qmd)).
- **`type = "cc"`, `sdY`** — bookkeeping that sets the *default prior*: for case-control
  (`"cc"`) coloc uses `sd.prior = 0.2`, i.e. $W = 0.04$
  ([Module 2](../02-a-prior-on-the-effect/lesson.qmd)); `sdY` (trait standard deviation)
  matters only for quantitative traits.
- **the returned `lABF` column** — your Module-3 closed form,
  $\tfrac12(\log(1 - r) + rZ^2)$, per SNP.
- **`beta`, `varbeta` in `coloc.abf`** — each study's summary pair per SNP; `varbeta` is the
  variance $V$, not the standard error.
- **the per-SNP posterior probability step** — within a region assumed to hold one causal
  variant, coloc turns the lABFs into probabilities by $e^{\text{lABF}}/\sum_j
  e^{\text{lABF}_j}$ (a normalized version of the posterior-odds step from
  [Module 4](../04-posterior-probability-of-association/lesson.qmd), with a flat prior over
  which SNP is causal).

Everything `coloc.abf` does with a single SNP's evidence, you can now reproduce. (Combining
*two* studies into the H0–H4 colocalisation hypotheses is the next course's job — see "what you
still can't read.")

## Assemble the whole method and validate it

Put the pieces together into the three functions the course built — `wakefield_labf`,
`wakefield_abf`, `wakefield_ppa` — and run them on a **simulated multi-SNP GWAS region**: six
SNPs, one of them the true causal signal.

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
# LIVE: the complete from-scratch method (base R), then a simulated GWAS region.
wakefield_labf <- function(z, V, W) { r <- W / (W + V); 0.5 * (log(1 - r) + r * z^2) }
wakefield_abf  <- function(beta, se, W) wakefield_labf(beta / se, se^2, W)
wakefield_ppa  <- function(labf, prior = 1e-4) { po <- prior/(1-prior) * exp(labf); po/(1+po) }

# A simulated region: 6 SNPs, se = 0.05 each, W = 0.04. SNP 3 is the strong signal.
snp  <- paste0("rs", 1:6)
beta <- c(0.02, -0.05, 0.21, 0.08, -0.03, 0.06)
se   <- rep(0.05, 6)
W    <- 0.04

lABF <- wakefield_abf(beta, se, W)
region_pp <- exp(lABF) / sum(exp(lABF))          # posterior prob WITHIN the region (one causal)

data.frame(snp, Z = beta / se, lABF = round(lABF, 3),
           region_pp = round(region_pp, 4))
# SNP 3 (Z = 4.2) carries lABF ~6.9 and ~99.8% of the regional posterior -- the causal hit
```

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
#| fig-alt: "A regional association plot: six SNPs on the horizontal axis, their posterior probability within the region on the vertical axis. Five SNPs sit near zero and SNP 3 towers near 1.0, marking it as the credible causal variant."
# LIVE: SEE the regional fine-mapping picture -- one SNP dominates the posterior.
wakefield_abf <- function(beta, se, W) { z <- beta/se; V <- se^2; r <- W/(W+V); 0.5*(log(1-r)+r*z^2) }
beta <- c(0.02, -0.05, 0.21, 0.08, -0.03, 0.06); se <- rep(0.05, 6); W <- 0.04
region_pp <- { l <- wakefield_abf(beta, se, W); exp(l) / sum(exp(l)) }

barplot(region_pp, names.arg = paste0("rs", 1:6), ylim = c(0, 1),
        ylab = "posterior prob. within region",
        main = "Which SNP is the signal? (one-causal fine-mapping)")
```

### The final self-check against `coloc` {#self-check}

One last time, prove the assembled method matches the real thing. The cell below embeds the
**frozen reference** from `equivalence/fixtures/wakefield-abf-full-coloc.json` — the exact
inputs and the `lABF` vector that `coloc::coloc.abf` 5.2.3 produced — and compares your
`wakefield_abf()` to them.

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
# LIVE FINAL SELF-CHECK: assembled method vs FROZEN coloc reference (tier: full).
wakefield_abf <- function(beta, se, W) { z <- beta/se; V <- se^2; r <- W/(W+V); 0.5*(log(1-r)+r*z^2) }

# frozen inputs + reference, copied verbatim from equivalence/fixtures/wakefield-abf-full-coloc.json
beta <- c(-0.02554337913844796, 0.008215202917039434, -0.005366809239341077, 0.16,
          0.03986549597916812, -0.05543162960131377, 0.005742157693248766,
          0.01323645801439164, -0.0438060912100528, -0.02261565535665145)
se   <- rep(0.03, 10)
W    <- 0.04
reference_labf <- c(-1.553741503528518, -1.871576145659659, -1.892595932433175,
                    12.001018497661109, -1.044752885297292, -0.238772015646558,
                    -1.890330388525183, -1.813051689371357, -0.8656081651460308,
                    -1.630349137649496)

mine <- wakefield_abf(beta, se, W)
max_abs_diff <- max(abs(mine - reference_labf))
cat("max |mine - coloc reference| =", format(max_abs_diff, scientific = TRUE), "\n")
cat("match within 1e-8?", max_abs_diff < 1e-8, "\n")    # TRUE
```

**Validation tier: FULL.** The match is against `coloc::coloc.abf` 5.2.3's actual output on
these inputs — the real reference library's numbers, not a reported figure or a re-derived
closed form — verified to about $10^{-8}$. This repo's `equivalence/check.R` re-verifies it in
CI. Your `wakefield_abf()` *is* coloc's ABF.

## Common traps

**Trap: "Wakefield's ABF and coloc's lABF should give the same big number for a strong hit."**
No — they use **opposite** sign conventions. For our causal SNP ($Z = 4.2$), coloc's lABF is
about $+6.9$ (large = strong), but Wakefield's own ABF is $e^{-6.9} \approx 0.001$ (small =
strong). Both say "strong association"; they just point in opposite directions. Reading a
Wakefield ABF of $0.001$ as "weak" would be exactly backwards — in his convention that tiny
number is overwhelming evidence.

**Trap: "the per-SNP regional posterior probability is the same as the genome-wide PPA."** No.
The regional $e^{\text{lABF}}/\sum_j e^{\text{lABF}_j}$ assumes *exactly one* causal SNP in the
region and a flat prior over which one — so it sums to 1 across the region ($0.998$ for our
SNP 3). The genome-wide PPA from [Module 4](../04-posterior-probability-of-association/lesson.qmd)
uses the tiny prior $\pi = 10^{-4}$ and does *not* sum to 1 — SNP 3's genome-wide PPA is only
about $0.089$. Different priors, different questions: "which SNP here?" vs "is this SNP real at
all?".

## What you can now do

- **Decode Wakefield's ABF equation** term by term, and translate between his reciprocal sign
  convention (small = strong) and coloc's (large = strong).
- **Read the `coloc::approx.bf.estimates` / `coloc.abf` interface** — its `z`, `V`, `type`,
  `sd.prior`, and the per-SNP regional posterior step — tracing each to the module that built
  it.
- **Assemble the full method** (`wakefield_labf`, `wakefield_abf`, `wakefield_ppa`) from
  scratch in base R and run it on a multi-SNP region to fine-map the likely causal variant.
- **Validate the from-scratch method** against the frozen `coloc::coloc.abf` 5.2.3 reference
  and state the validation tier (full).

If any field still reads as jargon, revisit the module it points to above — reading Wakefield's
ABF and the `coloc` interface cold is the entire goal of this course, and you just did it.

## What you still can't read (yet)

This course took you from a single association's summary statistics to its Bayes factor,
posterior probability, and a one-region fine-map. A few honest edges, each a natural next step:

- **Colocalisation of two traits (the H0–H4 hypotheses).** `coloc.abf` combines *two* studies'
  per-SNP ABFs to ask "do trait A and trait B share the same causal variant in this region?" —
  the five hypotheses H0 (no signal) through H4 (shared signal). That combination is built
  directly on the per-SNP ABF you now own; it is the subject of a **Bayesian Colocalisation**
  course.
- **SuSiE and multiple causal variants.** The one-causal assumption behind the regional
  posterior breaks when a region has several signals; newer methods (SuSiE-coloc) relax it.
- **How the summary statistics themselves were fitted.** The $(\hat\beta, \text{se})$ pair
  comes from a per-variant regression — the
  [logistic-regression course](../../../logistic-regression/syllabus.md) covers the
  single-variant case.

None of these change what you built; they extend it. You can read the core of Wakefield's ABF
and `coloc`'s per-SNP machinery now — the rest is more of the same shape.

## Where this goes next

You've built and decoded the Approximate Bayes Factor end to end. The natural continuation is
**Bayesian colocalisation** — using these per-SNP ABFs to test whether two traits share a
causal variant (the H0–H4 hypotheses of `coloc.abf`) — which reuses exactly what you now own.
Ready for the final cumulative set? Do the [practice problems](practice.qmd).
